revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:月光川]]


'''Gakkō River''' (月光川) is a {{ill|Class II river|ja|二級河川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class II river|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 二級水系|1=Class B river system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11371756}} in {{ill|Yuza|en|Yuza|qid=Q1347481|lt=Yuza, Yamagata|1=Yuza}}, {{ill|Akumi District, Yamagata|en|Akumi District, Yamagata|qid=Q1155592|lt=Akumi District, Yamagata|1=Akumi District, Yamagata}}, Yamagata Prefecture, Japan, flowing into the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|qid=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}. It is the main river of the Gakkō River system.

== Geography ==
The Gakkō River originates from the southern slopes of {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|qid=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai|1=Mount Chōkai}} in northeastern Yuza Town, Yamagata Prefecture. Flowing southwest, it turns north near {{ill|Yuza Station|en|Yuza Station|qid=Q8062241|lt=Yuza Station|1=Yuza Station}} on the {{ill|Uetsu Main Line|en|Uetsu Main Line|qid=Q1056211|lt=Uetsu Main Line|1=Uetsu Main Line}}, ultimately emptying into the Sea of Japan at Fukura, Yuza Town. Near its mouth, it is also called the Fukura River. The river is known as a spawning ground for salmon, and its clear waters, sourced from Mount Chōkai's springs, have supported artificial salmon hatching projects since {{nihongo|Meiji 40|明治40th year |1907}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masataka Suzuki|date=2005|url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN0006957X-00000061-0087|title=Salmon and Human Life History: A Case Study in Yuza Town, Akumi District, Yamagata Prefecture|journal=Keio Sociology Graduate School Bulletin|publisher=Keio University Graduate School of Sociology|volume=61|pages=87-108|ISSN=0912-456X}}</ref> Tributaries include {{ill|Araizawa River|ja|洗沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araizawa River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Ushiwatari River. In {{nihongo|1978|昭和53rd year }}, the Gakkō River Dam was constructed upstream for flood control and disaster prevention, with a total storage capacity of 1.67 million tons. Like {{ill|Tsugaruishi River|ja|津軽石川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsugaruishi River|1=Tsugaruishi River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17173615}} in {{ill|Miyako, Iwate|en|Miyako, Iwate|qid=Q502981|lt=Miyako, Iwate|1=Miyako, Iwate}}, this river is a well-known site for large salmon runs in autumn.

== History ==
The river's name originates from {{ill|Candraprabha|en|Candraprabha|qid=Q2671293|lt=Gakkō Bosatsu|1=Candraprabha}}, one of the attendants of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}. Since medieval times, the concept of {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honji suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} associated Mount Chōkai's deity {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} with Yakushi Nyorai as its honji (true form). Based on this belief, Mount Chōkai's two main rivers were identified with Gakkō Bosatsu and {{ill|Sūryaprabha|en|Sūryaprabha|qid=Q2661877|lt=Nikkō Bosatsu|1=Sūryaprabha}}, respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=Yasuaki Togawa|author-link={{ill|Yasuaki Togawa (folklorist)|ja|戸川安章 (民俗学者)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasuaki Togawa (folklorist)|1=Anshō Togawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11496356}}|title=Mount Haguro and Shugendō|publisher=Iwata Shoin|year=2005|series=Collected Works of Yasuaki Togawa|ISBN=4872943554}}</ref> Gakkō River corresponds to the present-day Gakkō River, while {{ill|Higashi River (Yamagata)|ja|日向川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi River (Yamagata)|1=Higashi River (Yamagata)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11505123}} (also called "Nikkō River") flows west into {{ill|Sakata, Yamagata|en|Sakata, Yamagata|qid=Q504690|lt=Sakata, Yamagata|1=Sakata, Yamagata}}.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=Togo Yoshida|title=The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Vol. 7: Ōu|publisher=Fuzanbo|year=1902|id="Nikkō River" section}}</ref>

By the Edo period, {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} practitioners and pilgrims frequented Mount Chōkai. The faith centered on the mountain emphasized water worship, viewing rivers as sacred. Pilgrimage routes to Mount Chōkai ascended from various points, including {{ill|Warabioka|ja|蕨岡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabioka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Fukura, and {{ill|Yashima, Akita|en|Yashima, Akita|qid=Q8049728|lt=Yashima|1=Yashima, Akita}}. Warabioka served as the headquarters of this religious activity, hosting 33 lodges and the academic temple {{ill|Ryūtō-ji|lt=Ryūtō-ji|lt=Ryuzuji Temple (Yuza Town, Yamagata Prefecture)|ja|龍頭寺 (山形県遊佐町)|Ryuzuji Temple (Yuza Town, Yamagata Prefecture)=Ryūtō-ji|lt=Ryuzuji Temple (Yuza Town, Yamagata Prefecture)|lt=Ryūtōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryuzuji Temple (Yuza Town, Yamagata Prefecture)|qq=}}.

During the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō era|1=Keichō}}, the river's midstream course was unstable. Sasaki Yoshitsuna of {{ill|Yoshide Village|ja|吉出村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshide Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} built embankments to stabilize the flow, reclaiming over 50 {{ill|chō|ja|町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Towns of Japan|qid=Q1059478}} of new rice paddies. To facilitate transportation of tax rice, a canal (now called Nishitō River) was dug to connect the Gakkō and Higashi Rivers, enabling efficient transport to the Sakata granary.

In {{nihongo|1807|文化4th year }}, flood control efforts included the construction of a new river channel, with laborers receiving rice stipends from the {{ill|Shōnai Domain|en|Shōnai Domain|qid=Q847303|lt=Shōnai Domain|1=Shōnai Domain}}. Additional work to prevent sand blockage at the mouth was carried out in {{nihongo|1824|文政7th year }}, supported by private funds.

In modern times, the river gained attention when the Gakkō River Park was featured in the 2008 film ''Departures'' ({{nihongo|Okuribito|おくりびと}}).

== Tributaries ==
* {{ill|Shōnai Takase River|ja|庄内高瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōnai Takase River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Araizawa River|ja|洗沢川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Araizawa River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ushiwatari River|ja|牛渡川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushiwatari River|1=Ushiwatari River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11570182}}

== Notes ==
{{reflist}}

== References ==
* Togo Yoshida, ''The Great Japanese Gazetteer, Vol. 7: Ōu,'' Fuzanbo, 1902.
* Kadokawa Dictionary Compilation Committee, edited by Takeuchi Rizo, ''Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary Vol. 6: Yamagata Prefecture,'' Kadokawa Shoten, 1981.
* Nagai Masataro, ''Japanese Historical Place Name Series Vol. 6: Yamagata Prefecture,'' Heibonsha, 1990.

== Related Articles ==
